note_data.title: The Periodic Table: chemical periodicity
keyPoints: **Periodicity** is the repetition of physical and chemical properties at regular intervals when elements are arranged in order of atomic number
**Atomic radius** decreases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge with same number of electron shells
**First ionisation energy** increases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge and decreasing atomic radius
**Metallic character** decreases across a period as elements change from metals to metalloids to non-metals
**Oxides** change from basic (metal oxides) to amphoteric to acidic (non-metal oxides) across a period
**Chlorides** change from ionic (metal chlorides) to covalent (non-metal chlorides) across a period
Group 1 elements show increasing reactivity down the group due to decreasing ionisation energy
Group 7 elements show decreasing reactivity down the group due to increasing atomic radius and shielding effect
**Melting points** across Period 3 increase from Na to Si then decrease from P to Ar due to changes in bonding and structure

definitions.definition: The repetition of physical and chemical properties at regular intervals when elements are arranged in order of atomic number
The energy required to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms to form one mole of gaseous 1+ ions
Half the distance between the nuclei of two atoms of the same element when they are covalently bonded together
An oxide that can react with both acids and bases to form salts

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the trend in one sentence without explanation
Give the trend + reason using nuclear charge, shielding, or atomic size
Use known periodic trends to suggest properties of unknown elements

modelAnswer.question: Explain the trend in atomic radius across Period 3 from sodium to chlorine.
modelAnswer.answer: Atomic radius decreases from sodium to chlorine. This is because nuclear charge increases across the period while the number of electron shells remains constant. The increased nuclear charge pulls the electrons closer to the nucleus, reducing the atomic radius.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Same number of electron shells/increased attraction pulls electrons closer

commonMistakes: Never say 'atomic size increases due to more protons' — nuclear charge increases but atomic radius decreases across periods
Students often confuse Group 1 reactivity (increases down group) with Group 7 reactivity (decreases down group)
Don't write 'atoms get bigger down groups due to more electrons' — say 'more electron shells/energy levels'
Never describe amphoteric as 'neutral' — it means reacts with both acids and bases
Students write 'covalent radius' instead of 'atomic radius' for bonded atoms
examinerTips: Periodic trends appear in every Paper 2 — learn the explanations using nuclear charge and shielding
Always state the trend first, then explain using electron configuration concepts
For oxide/chloride questions, link structure to properties (ionic vs covalent)
Group comparisons often test understanding of electron configuration changes
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Periodic table showing trends with arrows: atomic radius decreasing left to right, ionisation energy increasing left to right, metallic character decreasing left to right, and reactivity patterns for Groups 1 and 7
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the trend in atomic radius across Period 3 and down Group 1, with reference to nuclear charge and electron shielding
N/D 2022 P1 Q12 (2 marks) tested knowledge of ionization energy trends — students had to identify which element in Period 2 would have the highest first ionization energy and explain why
M/J 2022 P2 Q3(c) (4 marks) — candidates were required to explain why the melting point of sodium chloride is higher than sodium, linking to structure and bonding types
N/D 2023 P2 Q5 (5 marks) focused on periodicity of chemical properties — students explained why Group 1 elements become more reactive down the group using atomic structure

workedExample.question: Explain the trend in first ionization energy across Period 3 from sodium to argon. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: State the trend - first ionization energy generally increases across Period 3 from Na to Ar
Step 2: Explain nuclear charge increase - as we move across, nuclear charge increases from +11 to +18, attracting outer electrons more strongly
Step 3: Consider electron shielding - all elements have same number of inner electron shells (2), so shielding effect remains roughly constant
Step 4: Note exceptions - slight decrease at Al (3p¹ vs 3s²) and S (paired electrons in 3p⁴ repel)
workedExample.answer: First ionization energy increases across Period 3. Nuclear charge increases from +11 to +18, so outer electrons are more strongly attracted to the nucleus. Electron shielding remains constant as all elements have 2 inner shells. Small decreases occur at aluminum (3p orbital higher energy than 3s) and sulfur (electron-electron repulsion in paired 3p orbitals).
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct trend, 1 mark for nuclear charge explanation, 1 mark for shielding concept, 1 mark for mentioning exceptions or electron configuration effects

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused atomic radius with ionic radius' — always specify which type of radius you're discussing and remember atomic radius decreases across periods
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot to mention electron shielding when explaining ionization energy trends' — always include both nuclear charge AND shielding effects in your explanations
Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak responses failed to link periodicity to electron configuration' — always reference s, p, d subshells when explaining property changes across periods
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a periodic table showing the first 20 elements with clear period and group labels. Mark electron configurations for Period 3 elements (Na: 2,8,1 through Ar: 2,8,8). Include arrows showing trends: atomic radius decreasing left→right and increasing top→bottom; ionization energy increasing left→right and bottom→top. Label s-block (Groups 1-2) and p-block (Groups 3-8) regions clearly.
note_data.quickRecap: Periodicity means properties repeat in patterns across periods. Across periods: atomic radius decreases, ionization energy increases (due to increasing nuclear charge, constant shielding). Down groups: atomic radius increases, ionization energy decreases (more electron shells = more shielding). Chemical properties depend on outer electron configuration - same group = similar properties.
